cards.title: Kythik’s
cards.subtitle: Human value route
cards.summary: Default normal-board route. Build a strong chest package, upgrade real targets, and avoid cute detours that waste movement.
details: Core rule: Improve the final chest package, not just the current tile.
Chest priority: Take a good chest now if it does not block better chest quality later.
Upgrade priority: Spend upgrades on chests that actually matter; do not save them forever waiting for a miracle.
Pathing priority: Take movement, card, or ? tiles when they create access to better chests or upgrades.
Common mistake: Do not fill low chests just because a chest is available. A bad filled slot can be worse than waiting.
playPattern: Open by looking for chest access plus support tiles, not just the nearest chest.
Ask: “Does this improve my final seven, or am I just filling space?”
Cash in the clean line once the remaining board is mostly empty, trap-heavy, or too far away.
cards.profile: Use this when you want the route a good human would actually play. Your first priority is not “get seven chests”; it is “do not waste chest slots on trash when better chests are still reachable.” Take early chests when they are efficient, but leave room for purple/gold/red/rainbow if the board is clearly giving you access. Cards and ? tiles are good when they help you reach or upgrade better chests; they are bad when they only delay you.
cards.cardSummary: Practical human route: protect chest slots, upgrade real targets, and do not chase cute lines that waste movement.
notes: Take a good chest now if it does not block better chest quality later.
Take movement/cards/? tiles when they create access to better chests or upgrades.
Use upgrades on chests that will actually matter; do not save upgrades forever waiting for a miracle.
Do not fill low chests just because a chest is available. A bad filled slot can be worse than waiting.
When you already have a strong chest package or the remaining path is mostly empty/trap/low-value mystery, cash in the clean line.

walkthrough.heading: Build the package
steps: Start with the best reachable chest path, then check what cards and ? tiles support it.
Take strong chests early when they do not block better future options.
Avoid low-value chest filler unless it protects the final package.

walkthrough.heading: Upgrade real targets
steps: Use upgrades when the target is already good enough or clearly reachable.
Do not hoard upgrades for a perfect target that may never appear.
Use movement and cards to create access to better upgrade targets.

walkthrough.heading: Stop the cute line
steps: Stop greeding when the remaining path is mostly empty, traps, or low-value mystery.
Protect the clean finish instead of chasing one more maybe-payoff.
The goal is a strong final package, not a board that looks perfectly solved.

cards.whenToUse: Use as the normal-board default when you want practical, human-readable value routing.
cards.whenToAvoid: Avoid only when you are specifically chasing fastest average value or a pure seven-chest completion line.

cards.title: Best Found
cards.subtitle: Value-tempo route
cards.summary: Fast value route. Convert movement into chest value early, spend upgrades sooner, and cut off late detours before they tax the run.
details: Core rule: Take efficient value now instead of over-planning a perfect board.
Tempo priority: Convert movement into chest value quickly; wandering is usually the hidden tax.
Upgrade priority: Spend upgrade cards when they improve a good target now or in the next reachable band.
Pathing priority: Avoid empty tiles harder than feels natural.
Common mistake: Do not chase late ? tiles unless they are cheap and directly improve the finish.
playPattern: Take efficient early value instead of trying to make the board look clean.
Use upgrades sooner when the target is already good enough.
Cut off late detours quickly if they do not convert into chest value.
cards.profile: Use this when you want the strongest tested expected value, even if the line looks weird. This route is more willing to spend movement and upgrades aggressively because it cares about converting the board into value quickly. It hates empty tiles, likes upgrade/card tempo, and stops chasing ? tiles earlier than a human might. The main rule is: if a move does not create chest value soon, the solver probably does not want it.
cards.cardSummary: Highest EV route: aggressive value tempo, early conversion, hard empty-tile avoidance, fewer cute late gambles.
notes: Convert movement into chest value quickly. Do not wander for theoretical future upside.
Spend upgrade cards when they create real value now or in the next reachable chest band.
Avoid empty tiles harder than feels natural; empty movement is usually the hidden tax that kills EV.
Do not chase late ? tiles unless they are cheap and directly improve the finish.
This can look unintuitive because it is not trying to make the board look clean; it is trying to win the average.

walkthrough.heading: Take tempo
steps: Look for the fastest route that turns movement into real chest value.
Prefer clean early value over a perfect long setup.
Do not spend multiple moves setting up a reward that is only slightly better.

walkthrough.heading: Upgrade sooner
steps: Upgrade good-enough targets when they are already secured or easy to secure.
Do not wait for a miracle chest if a strong target is available now.
Use cards to support the next reachable value band, not a fantasy end state.

walkthrough.heading: Kill bad detours
steps: Treat empty movement as a real cost.
Skip late ? tiles unless they are cheap and directly improve the finish.
End with efficient value instead of a half-finished greed route.

cards.whenToUse: Use when you want the strongest average-value normal-board route and are comfortable with faster decisions.
cards.whenToAvoid: Avoid when you prefer a more conservative human route or are specifically forcing seven-chest completion.

cards.title: 7 Chests → Rarity
cards.subtitle: Completion route
cards.summary: Completion-focused route. Keep seven chests realistic, take chest access earlier, then improve rarity when the cost is reasonable.
details: Core rule: Keep the seven-chest finish alive without completely ignoring rarity.
Chest priority: Take chest count earlier than the other routes when future access is uncertain.
Pathing priority: Protect the route to chest six and seven.
Upgrade priority: Still prefer upgraded or higher-rarity chests when the cost is reasonable.
Common mistake: Do not chase perfect rarity so long that you finish with only four or five chests.
playPattern: Check whether seven chests is realistic from the first few moves.
Protect access to the sixth and seventh chest.
Once seven chests is nearly secured, stop being picky and complete the package.
cards.profile: Use this when the goal is to complete seven chests more often. This route still cares about rarity, but it is more willing to secure chest count earlier. The human rule is: keep the seven-chest finish alive, but do not throw away the run by stuffing every green/blue chest immediately. It is the safest pick when missing the seventh chest feels worse than losing some average value.
cards.cardSummary: Completion route: keep seven chests alive, take chest access earlier, but do not completely abandon rarity.
notes: Take chest count earlier than the other strategies, especially if future access is uncertain.
Still prefer upgraded or higher-rarity chests when the cost is reasonable.
Do not chase perfect rarity so long that you end with only four or five chests.
Once 7 chests is nearly secured, stop being picky and complete the package.

walkthrough.heading: Count the route
steps: Start by asking whether seven chests is actually reachable.
Prioritize paths that keep chest six and seven alive.
Take earlier chest bodies when waiting could close the completion line.

walkthrough.heading: Add rarity carefully
steps: Upgrade or favor higher-rarity chests when the cost is reasonable.
Do not sacrifice the seventh chest for a tiny rarity improvement.
Use movement cards to preserve completion access first, then improve quality.

walkthrough.heading: Finish the package
steps: Once seven chests is close, stop being overly picky.
Secure the final chest if the cost is reasonable.
This route wins by finishing the package, not by chasing the prettiest possible board.

cards.whenToUse: Use when the board shows a realistic seven-chest line and you want completion pressure.
cards.whenToAvoid: Avoid when the seven-chest line is fake, too expensive, or blocks clearly better upgraded chest value.
